title: Factor analysis of mixed (numeric + categorical) data
summary: FAMD in the FactoMineR tradition. For each batch defined in meta, fit one mixed-data model per n_factors in [min_count, max_count] and emit a single .xlsx workbook. Each batch gets a `<batch>_sum` sheet (eigenvalues across k), a `<batch>_vars` sheet (per-variable coord/contrib/cos² across k), and — when the batch contains categorical variables — a `<batch>_cats` sheet (per-level detail across k).
description: Pipeline per (batch, k): complete-case filter; split into numeric and qualitative columns; drop zero-variance numerics and single-level factors; standardise numerics with n-1 SD; disjunctively code categoricals as (indicator - proportion)/sqrt(proportion); concatenate and take the thin SVD of X/√n. Dimension coordinates are σ·V, contributions are V²·100 (%), cos² is coord²/total per item.
usage:
[["factor-analysis::famd"]]
input = "./data.sav"
run_dir = "./runs"
run_name = "famd1"
min_count = 2
max_count = 5

meta = [
  { match = "^q\\d+$", batch = "attitudes", type = "ordinal" },
  { variable = "gender", batch = "demographics", type = "categorical" },
]

arguments.name: input
arguments.description: Path to the input file. `.csv` / `.tsv` use the CSV reader; `.sav` / `.zsav` use the SPSS reader.

arguments.name: meta
arguments.description: Variable metadata: either a path to a meta CSV, or an inline TOML list of row/pattern entries. See the Inline meta notes below.

arguments.name: run_dir
arguments.description: Base directory for outputs. Files land in `<run_dir>/ocinfo/<run_name>_factor_analysis/`.

arguments.name: run_name
arguments.description: Short identifier for this run; no `..`, `/`, or `\`.

arguments.name: batches
arguments.description: Subset of batch names to analyse. Empty = all batches from meta (or a single `all` batch if meta has no batch column).

arguments.name: min_count
arguments.description: Smallest number of dimensions per batch.

arguments.name: max_count
arguments.description: Largest number of dimensions per batch.

arguments.name: na_action
arguments.description: `complete_cases` (listwise) or `fail`. FAMD is always listwise per batch.

arguments.name: headers
arguments.description: Whether CSV inputs have a header row. Ignored for `.sav` inputs.

arguments.name: threads
arguments.description: Worker thread count. `1` runs serially. Results are deterministic regardless of thread count.

examples.title: CSV with external meta CSV
examples.body:
[["factor-analysis::famd"]]
input = "./data.csv"
meta = "./meta.csv"
run_dir = "./runs"
run_name = "famd1"
min_count = 2
max_count = 5

examples.title: Output: <batch>_sum sheet
examples.body:
One row per (n_factors, eigenvalue dim). Header cells:
  n_factors | n_obs | dim | eigenvalue | prop | cumulative

examples.title: Output: <batch>_vars sheet
examples.body:
One row per (n_factors, variable). coord/contrib/cos² columns widen to the largest k in the batch; rows with smaller k leave higher-dim columns blank.
  n_factors | variable | label | is_categorical
  | coord_dim1 .. coord_dim<max_k>
  | contrib_dim1 .. contrib_dim<max_k>
  | cos2_dim1 .. cos2_dim<max_k>
  | strongest_dim | strongest_abs_coord

examples.title: Output: <batch>_cats sheet (only when categoricals exist)
examples.body: Same columns as <batch>_vars but with an extra `level` column and one row per (n_factors, variable, level). Numeric variables do not appear.

notes: Inline meta semantics: entries are evaluated in order, all-matches-apply. A column hit by multiple pattern entries is added once per entry, so one column can belong to multiple batches. Unmatched columns are silently dropped. Row entries use `variable = "..."`; pattern entries use `match = "<regex>"`.

Label templates (pattern entries): `label_template = "Satisfaction: ${1}"` expands `${name}` (whole column name), `${0}` (whole regex match), and `${1}`, `${2}`, ... (positional captures). Omitted template → SPSS variable label (`.sav`) or column name (CSV).
Column `type` drives the encoding: numeric is standardised; dichotomous, ordinal, and categorical are disjunctively coded. Mixed batches (numeric + categorical) are the main use case.
Variables with zero variance (numeric) or a single level (categorical) are dropped from a batch with a warning.
`strongest_dim` / `strongest_abs_coord` identify the axis on which each variable or level loads most heavily — useful as a secondary sort when exploring results.
